attributes.stem:
A 60-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-week history of weakness in his right arm. He has also experienced dry eyes and difficulty swallowing over the past three months. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Examination of the right arm reveals fasciculations, increased tone without clonus, and power of 4/5 in all movements. Biceps and triceps reflexes are brisk. Sensation in the right upper limb is intact. Neurological examination of the left arm and lower limbs is normal. Cranial nerve examination shows incomplete closure of the right eye and asymmetrical raising of the eyebrows, with normal extraocular movements.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs in an asymmetric distribution, along with bulbar symptoms. Motor neuron disease, specifically amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), characteristically presents with this mixed picture. Upper motor neuron signs include increased tone and brisk reflexes, while lower motor neuron signs include fasciculations and weakness. The presence of bulbar symptoms (dysphagia) and facial weakness further supports this diagnosis.

Vitamin B12 deficiency typically presents with symmetrical peripheral neuropathy (causing lower motor neuron signs), posterior column dysfunction (loss of vibration and proprioception), and neuropsychiatric features. The asymmetric presentation and presence of upper motor neuron signs make this diagnosis unlikely.

Spinal cord transection would present with a clear sensory level and bilateral neurological findings below the level of injury. The preservation of lower limb function and asymmetric presentation excludes this diagnosis.

Cerebellar infarction typically presents with ataxia, nystagmus, and hypotonia rather than increased tone. The absence of these features and presence of both upper and lower motor neuron signs makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Alzheimer's disease primarily presents with progressive cognitive decline and behavioural changes. Motor symptoms are not typically present early in the disease course.
learningPoints: Motor neuron disease presents with mixed upper and lower motor neuron symptoms and signs, progressing to impair bulbar, autonomic, respiratory and cognitive function.
The most common presenting symptom for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is asymmetrical limb weakness.
Presence of both upper motor neuron (increased tone, hyperreflexia) and lower motor neuron (fasciculations, weakness) signs is characteristic of ALS.
references: Hardiman O, Al-Chalabi A, Chio A, Corr EM, Logroscino G, Robberecht W, Shaw PJ, Simmons Z, van den Berg LH. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2017 Oct 5;3:17071.  Available at: https://www.nature.com/articles/nrdp201771
